---
title: "Portable Polarization: Partisan Identity, Expressive Attitudes, and COVID-19 Health Behavior"
author:
  - name: Chris Soria
    affiliations:
      - name: University of California, Berkeley
        department: Department of Demography
    email: chrissoria@berkeley.edu
    corresponding: true
date: today
abstract: |
  This study examines partisan differences in health behaviors during the COVID-19 pandemic using data from the Berkeley Interpersonal Contact Study (BICS). We analyze how political affiliation shaped individual responses to the pandemic, including social contact patterns, mask-wearing behavior, and vaccination uptake. Our findings reveal substantial and persistent partisan gaps in protective health behaviors that varied across geographic contexts and evolved over the course of the pandemic. These results have important implications for understanding how political polarization influences public health outcomes and for designing effective health communication strategies.
keywords:
  - COVID-19
  - political polarization
  - health behaviors
  - partisanship
  - public health
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    fontsize: 12pt
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 1.5
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
linestretch: 1.5
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)
```

{{< include sections/introduction.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/methods.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/results.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/discussion.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/conclusion.qmd >}}

# References

::: {#refs}
:::

{{< include sections/appendix.qmd >}}
